ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Tarka Dal. Nearest parent is a cooked lentil dish or legume-based curry. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume dishes — cooked yellow lentil (dal) with spice tempering (closest parent)

main_ingredients: toor dal (split pigeon peas) or chana dal (split chickpeas)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical bowl of Dal Curry served at a Singapore Indian stall weighs approximately 250g and consists of soft-cooked yellow lentils in a mildly spiced, fragrant broth tempered with ghee or oil, cumin seeds, and onion, accompanied as a side dish with rice or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; HPB Food Code G00011178
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad; HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Foodo under food code G00011178 — Dal Curry, yellow lentil preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — yellow split peas / pigeon peas (cooked); lentils have one of the lowest GIs of all carbohydrate foods due to high fibre and resistant starch.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 705mg per bowl (250g) — about 31% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; moderate sodium for a flavourful curry dish.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume dal curry regularly; it is a low-fat, high-fibre, moderate-sodium side dish that supports healthy blood pressure when substituted for higher-fat, higher-sodium alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low GI (~29) from the lentil base; glycaemic load per bowl of ~10 is very low; high dietary fibre and plant protein moderate postprandial glucose exceptionally well.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Curry is one of the best side dish choices for diabetic patients; its very low GI, high fibre, and plant protein content significantly improve the overall glycaemic profile of any meal it accompanies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate content supports immune regulation; turmeric, cumin, and coriander in tarka provide anti-inflammatory compounds; plant protein from lentils supports muscle maintenance.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Curry is an excellent dietary staple for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the high folate, anti-inflammatory spice tempering, and plant-based protein make it one of the most beneficial daily foods for inflammatory conditions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Curry (Tarka Dal) is a foundational Indian dish of slow-cooked yellow lentils (typically toor dal or chana dal) simmered until soft in a mildly spiced broth with turmeric, tomatoes, and onions, then finished with a tarka — a hot oil or ghee tempering of cumin seeds, garlic, dried chillies, and curry leaves poured sizzling over the dal. It is served as a side dish alongside rice, roti, thosai, or prata, and is one of the most nutritionally dense, high-protein, high-fibre, and low-GI items in Singapore's Indian hawker repertoire.
common_user_queries: how many calories in dal curry
is dal curry high in protein
dhal curry vs sambar which is healthier

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code G00011178 — Dal Curry, yellow lentil preparation.
notes: Vitamin B12 is 0.0 in a vegan version (no ghee); versions with ghee will have trace B12. Folate content is notably high from lentils and is a clinically significant value. The dish is inherently gluten-free and typically halal. When made with ghee, it contains trace dairy and is not vegan.
